LATAM becomes first South American carrier to roll out Wi-Fi on long-haul routes

LATAM Airlines has completed its first long-haul flight equipped with internet connectivity. The new service was introduced on…...

LATAM's Wi-Fi deployment on long-haul routes signals accelerating connectivity investments in emerging markets, potentially reshaping competitive dynamics in Latin American aviation.

Philippine Airlines profits rise to $160 million in 2025 despite higher costs

Philippine Airlines (PAL) closed 2025 with a net income of $160.4 million, a 6.1% increase from the previous…...

PAL's profit growth outpaced its cost increases, suggesting improved operational efficiency and pricing power in competitive Asian markets.

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Creating near-term lunar settlements: lessons from space history

March 16, 2026, was the 100th anniversary of Robert Goddard’s first flight of a liquid fueled rocket. It reached an altitude of 41 feet. 31 years later, in 1957, Sputnik began a lonely beep as the first satellite in orbit. In 1969, 12 years after Sputnik and 43 years after Goddard’s first flight,...

Historical milestones show that major spaceflight achievements required 40+ years from foundational research to operational capability, suggesting realistic lunar settlement timelines may extend beyond current industry projections.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

WB-57 and Secretive RAT-55 Spotted Rehearsing for NASA’s Artemis Launch

The secretive U.S. Air Force NT-43A RAT55 was spotted flying with a NASA callsign in one of the areas reserved for the launch of the Artemis II mission, opposite to the one used by the WB-57 Canberra. NASA is set to launch the Artemis II mission on Apr. 1, 2026, at 6:24 pm EDT (22:24 […]...

NASA's Artemis II lunar mission requires specialized atmospheric monitoring aircraft like the WB-57 and classified RAT-55 to collect real-time data during launch operations, indicating the complexity of modern spaceflight support infrastructure.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 1, 2026

Artemis 2 fueling underway

Fueling of the SLS is underway for an April 1 launch attempt of Artemis 2, the first mission to send humans toward the moon in more than 50 years.

Artemis 2's fueling milestone signals NASA's imminent return to lunar exploration, representing a critical step toward establishing sustainable human spaceflight beyond Earth orbit.

See SpaceNews →

Regulatory Apr 1, 2026

Boeing's Higher MTOW 787 Dreamliners Cleared By FAA

While we’ve already covered some of this news through our March 9th video, it was only on March 23rd that the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) certified Boeing’s increased maximum takeoff weights (iMTOWs) for the 787-9 and 787-10.

Boeing's newly certified higher MTOW variants enable 787s to carry additional payload or fuel, directly increasing operational efficiency and revenue-generating capacity for carriers.

See Simple Flying →

Part 5 compliance requires systematic hazard identification and mitigation planning rather than reactive safety measures, fundamentally shifting operational risk management strategies.

KSQL Noise Abatement Departure

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo,...

San Carlos Airport's noise-sensitive location within Bay Area Class B airspace requires specialized departure procedures balancing community relations with operational efficiency.

Commercial Apr 1, 2026

Why the 787-10 uses fewer wheels than the A350-1000

Why In The World Does The Boeing 787-10 Dreamliner Have Fewer Wheels Than The Airbus A350-1000?At first glance, the landing gear of modern widebody aircraft can look almost identical. Large twin-engine jets sit low on the ground, supported by multiple wheels arranged in similar patterns, and unless...

The 787-10's lighter composite structure allows it to distribute loads more efficiently, requiring fewer landing gear wheels than the heavier A350-1000 despite similar cabin sizes.

See Simple Flying →

Air Cargo Apr 1, 2026

Why Do Cargo Carriers Still Avoid Twin-Engine Replacements?

Older jets can still be highly valuable if they offer the right mix of range, volume, and operating economics.

Legacy cargo operators prioritize proven operating economics and existing supply chains over newer twins, making replacement investment unattractive until fuel costs or maintenance escalate significantly.

Extended pre-flight timelines create unexpected dynamics for astronaut crews, requiring sustained public engagement and team cohesion despite extended delays.

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Wire strikes remain lethal despite pilots overestimating their reaction time, requiring systemic detection and avoidance technologies rather than relying on pilot awareness alone.

Industry Apr 1, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across the full aviation system—not just individual aircraft—is critical to meeting capacity growth while reducing environmental impact.

With over a thousand F-35s built and thousands more on order, the F-35's flyaway costs can be lower than some fourth-generation fighter jets.

High-volume F-35 production achieves economies of scale that make fifth-generation fighter costs competitive with legacy fourth-generation platforms, reshaping military aircraft procurement economics.

The 787-10's lighter composite structure allows it to distribute loads more efficiently, requiring fewer landing gear wheels than the heavier A350-1000 despite similar cabin sizes.
